cumba 0.0.1.0

    • Run a scenario (cumba_scenario)
    • Calibrate ET0 and radiation

Run a deficit-irrigation scenario with cumba_scenario()

Source: vignettes/cumba-scenario.Rmd
cumba-scenario.Rmd

Why cumba_scenario()?

While cumba_experiment() reproduces an observed irrigation schedule, cumba_scenario() runs the model in automatic deficit-irrigation mode. Irrigation is triggered when, simultaneously:

  1. the simulated water-stress index ws falls below a phase-specific threshold (waterStressLevel), and
  2. at least minimumTurn days have elapsed since the previous wetting.

The amount of water applied refills the soil to field capacity in the rooted layers.

This is the function to use for what-if studies, irrigation strategy design, and climate-impact assessments.

Phase-specific stress thresholds and turns

waterStressLevel and minimumTurn are named lists with one entry per phenological phase:

  • vegetative — from transplanting to flowering,
  • reproductive — from flowering to mid-ripening,
  • ripening — from mid-ripening to harvest.

Typical defaults:

library(cumba)

waterStressLevel <- list(vegetative = 0.5,
                         reproductive = 0.5,
                         ripening = 0.5)

minimumTurn <- list(vegetative = 4,
                    reproductive = 4,
                    ripening = 4)

Lower thresholds (e.g. 0.3) impose more deficit; higher thresholds (e.g. 0.7) imply more frequent watering.

A complete walkthrough on tomatoFoggia 

library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

data(tomatoFoggia)
weather <- tomatoFoggia$weather

out <- cumba_scenario(
  weather          = weather,
  param            = cumbaParameters,
  estimateRad      = TRUE,
  estimateET0      = TRUE,
  transplantingDOY = 120,
  waterStressLevel = waterStressLevel,
  minimumTurn      = minimumTurn,
  fullOut          = FALSE
)

head(out)

Compare two irrigation strategies

Run a full and a deficit scenario and compare yield and Brix at harvest:

full_irr <- list(vegetative = 0.7, reproductive = 0.7, ripening = 0.6)
deficit  <- list(vegetative = 0.4, reproductive = 0.4, ripening = 0.3)

run_strategy <- function(level, label) {
  cumba_scenario(
    weather          = weather,
    param            = cumbaParameters,
    waterStressLevel = level,
    minimumTurn      = minimumTurn,
    transplantingDOY = 120,
    fullOut          = FALSE
  ) |>
    mutate(strategy = label)
}

scen <- bind_rows(
  run_strategy(full_irr, "Full irrigation"),
  run_strategy(deficit,  "Deficit irrigation")
)

harvest <- scen |>
  group_by(strategy, site, year) |>
  slice_tail(n = 1) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(strategy, site, year, yield, brix)

ggplot(harvest, aes(strategy, yield, fill = strategy)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Yield (q ha-1)", x = NULL,
       title = "Yield response to irrigation strategy") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

ggplot(harvest, aes(strategy, brix, fill = strategy)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(y = "Brix (°)", x = NULL,
       title = "Brix response to irrigation strategy") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

Daily soil-water and irrigation events 

ggplot(scen |> filter(year == min(year)),
       aes(doy, swc, colour = strategy)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point(aes(y = irrigation), size = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "DOY", y = "Soil water content (%) / Irrigation (mm)",
       title = "Soil water dynamics under two strategies")

Notes

  • cumba_scenario() does not require an irrigation_df: the schedule is generated internally from the stress thresholds.
  • The simulation window starts at transplantingDOY and ends at most 150 days later (typical processing-tomato cycle length).
  • The crop coefficient (Kc) is computed exactly as in cumba_experiment(): it follows kcIni until full canopy, then decreases linearly during senescence based on the achieved maximum light interception. This guarantees that yield and Brix predictions are directly comparable between experiment and scenario runs.
